title: Metrics Aren't Perfect Judges
concept: IS and FID have blind spots, can be fooled by texture artifacts, and do not perfectly align with human visual judgment, meaning they are guides rather than absolute truth.

exercises.explain: While Inception Score (IS) and Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) are incredibly helpful, they can sometimes be tricked by strange patterns that humans instantly spot as fake.
exercises.prompt: What is a major limitation of AI image evaluation metrics like IS and FID?

options.text: They are 100% accurate and always match human eyes.
They have blind spots and can be fooled by strange textures.
They only work on black and white drawings.

cards.body: Imagine a painting contest judged by a robot that only counts colors. A messy splash of paint might get a perfect score, while a beautiful, hand-drawn portrait gets a low one. The same thing happens with AI image metrics!
cards.title: The Robot Art Judge

cards.body: AI metrics like IS (Inception Score) and FID (Fréchet Inception Distance) measure image quality by comparing math features. However, they have 'blind spots' where they grade weird, distorted images as perfect just because the textures look technically correct.
cards.title: Blind Spots in Metrics

cards.body: Think of these metrics like a spell-checker. A spell-checker can tell you if every word is spelled correctly, but it cannot tell you if your story is actually interesting, funny, or makes any logical sense.
cards.title: The Spell-Checker Effect

cards.body: An AI generates an image of a dog with six legs, but the fur texture looks incredibly realistic. Because the texture is perfect, FID gives it a fantastic score, completely ignoring the fact that the dog's structure is totally wrong!
cards.title: The Realistic Six-Legged Dog
Lower Score = Perfect Image?
cards.body: Many believe that if an AI model achieves a record-low FID score, it must generate the best looking images. In reality, a model can cheat the metric by producing repetitive patterns that look good to a computer but terrible to us.
Let's compare how humans and math metrics judge image quality.

left.text: Looks at overall realism, logic, shapes, and structural correctness.

right.text: Looks at mathematical distribution, color patches, and texture statistics.

cards.title: Human vs. Metric Judgment

exercises.prompt: Why do AI metrics sometimes disagree with human judgment?
exercises.explain: Metrics like FID are great guides, but they look at mathematical statistics rather than understanding the visual logic (like checking if a face has two eyes).

options.text: They look at statistical patterns rather than logical structure.
They only measure how fast the computer runs.
They can tell if a story in an image is emotionally moving.

exercises.explain: True! An image can have a wonderful FID score but still look strange and distorted to a human judge because of structural errors.
exercises.prompt: An image with an excellent FID score can still look completely unrealistic to a human.

exercises.explain: Texture artifacts can easily trick mathematical metrics into giving a good score, even if the overall image shape is wrong.
exercises.sentence: AI metrics can be fooled by texture ___ that look statistically correct but look fake to humans.

exercises.explain: The cat with two tails is a classic structural error that math-based metrics often miss because the fur texture itself looks very convincing.
exercises.prompt: Which scenario represents a metric blind spot where a bad AI image gets a good score?

options.text: An AI-generated cat image with realistic fur texture but two distinct tails.
A blurry photo of a cat taken with an old digital camera.

exercises.prompt: Since AI metrics aren't perfect judges, how should we use them when training GANs?

options.text: Throw away all mathematical metrics and only use human voting.
Use metrics as helpful guides, but use human eyes for the final word on realism.
Trust the mathematical scores completely without ever looking at the images.

exercises.explain: AI metrics should be used as helpful guides, but human evaluation remains the gold standard for final visual quality. In the next lesson, we will explore how to combine both!
